metadata.visit_title: GI clinic — pre-procedure consult for urgent ERCP in a pregnant patient with symptomatic choledocholithiasis

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: ERCP order routed to routine outpatient endoscopy queue (next available ~5 weeks)
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: referral specifies urgent-tier ERCP; symptomatic choledocholithiasis with rising LFTs in a 24-week pregnancy is time-sensitive
planted_discrepancies.note: must be pulled from routine queue and expedited with fluoro-minimization protocol and OB-experienced anesthesia/MFM coordination

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: medication list shows prenatal vitamins only
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient also takes low-dose aspirin 81 mg daily, started by MFM for preeclampsia prophylaxis
planted_discrepancies.note: relevant to sphincterotomy bleeding risk and periprocedural planning; prophylactic (not therapeutic) dose

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient describes the aspirin as her 'blood thinner'; it is prophylactic low-dose aspirin, not a therapeutic anticoagulant
planted_discrepancies.note: hedged patient framing risks over-restriction; low-dose ASA generally need not be held for ERCP/sphincterotomy — clarify rather than assume therapeutic anticoagulation

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient reports itching and hives during a prior CT with IV iodinated contrast
planted_discrepancies.note: ERCP uses iodinated contrast cholangiography; premedication vs. minimized-contrast technique must be planned

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: no fever/rigors documented; referral notes biliary colic with rising LFTs
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient reports one episode of chills and a subjective fever last night with RUQ pain
planted_discrepancies.note: raises concern for evolving ascending cholangitis (Charcot triad features) — further escalates urgency of ERCP

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient confirms she is 24 weeks pregnant and seeing MFM
patient confirms she takes her prenatal vitamin daily

prior_notes.text: G2P1 at 22 weeks gestation by first-trimester dating. Singleton, anatomy survey normal. BP mildly elevated on two prior visits; started low-dose aspirin 81 mg daily for preeclampsia prophylaxis, to continue through 36 weeks. Reports two episodes of severe postprandial right upper quadrant pain over the past two weeks — GI/hepatobiliary referral placed for evaluation of known gallstones. Advised to present for fever, persistent pain, jaundice, or dark urine. Continue prenatal vitamin.

24-week pregnant female with recurrent RUQ pain radiating to back, associated nausea, following a fatty meal. RUQ ultrasound: cholelithiasis with a dilated common bile duct (7 mm) and echogenic focus suggestive of a distal CBD stone. LFTs elevated and rising compared with outpatient baseline (total bilirubin 2.4 mg/dL, ALT 210 U/L). Afebrile in department, hemodynamically stable. Symptoms improved with hydration and analgesia. Discharged with expedited GI referral for consideration of ERCP; MFM aware. Return precautions for fever, worsening pain, or jaundice.

MedicationRequest._discrepancy: C2: confirmed by patient. NOTE: chart is missing low-dose aspirin 81 mg (D2) that patient reports taking daily per MFM.

code.text: ERCP (urgent, planned) — fluoro-minimization protocol in pregnancy

DiagnosticReport.conclusion: Cholelithiasis. Dilated common bile duct measuring 7 mm with an echogenic focus in the distal CBD suggestive of choledocholithiasis. No gallbladder wall thickening or pericholecystic fluid. Gravid uterus, single intrauterine pregnancy.

transcript:
DR: Hi Priya, thanks for coming in. I'm the GI doctor — we're meeting to plan a procedure called an ERCP to deal with the gallstone that's blocking your bile duct. First things first, how far along are you?
MA: Before we get into it — I just roomed you and took your vitals: you're 5 foot 4, 152 pounds, temp 98.6, blood pressure 132 over 84, heart rate 88, breathing 16, and your oxygen's 98 percent on room air.
PT: I'm 24 weeks now. My MFM doctor knows I'm here — she's the one who's been managing everything.
DR: Good, and we'll keep her in the loop closely. Tell me what's been happening.
PT: It started a few weeks ago — really bad pain on the right side under my ribs after I eat, especially anything greasy. Then last week it got worse, I went to the ER, and they said a stone had moved into the duct. My blood tests for the liver went up.
DR: That's right — your bilirubin and liver numbers are elevated and climbing, which is why we don't want to wait. Anything new since the ER?
PT: Actually, yes — last night I had chills, I was shaking, and I felt feverish for a bit along with the pain. It settled by morning but it scared me.
DR: I'm really glad you mentioned that. Chills and fever with this kind of blockage is something we take seriously. Let me go through your medicines. The chart shows a prenatal vitamin — are you taking anything else?
PT: Yes — my prenatal every day. And my MFM started me on a baby aspirin, the 81, for my blood pressure, to prevent preeclampsia. I think of it as my blood thinner.
DR: That's important, thank you — it's not on our list. To be clear, that low-dose aspirin isn't a true blood thinner like warfarin; for this procedure we usually don't even need to stop it, but I'll confirm with the team.
PT: Oh, okay, that's a relief.
DR: Any allergies?
PT: I don't think it's written down, but during a CT scan a couple years ago the dye made me itch and break out in hives.
DR: That matters here — the ERCP uses an X-ray dye too, so we'll plan for that and keep the X-ray exposure to an absolute minimum to protect the baby. I'm going to get you scheduled urgently with an anesthesia team that's experienced with pregnancy, not in the routine line. We'll coordinate with your MFM.
PT: Thank you. I just want this handled safely.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Raghunathan is a 33-year-old woman (G2P1) at 24 weeks gestation presenting to GI clinic for pre-procedure consultation regarding urgent ERCP for symptomatic choledocholithiasis. She describes several weeks of severe postprandial right upper quadrant pain; an ER visit on 2026-07-14 identified a dilated common bile duct with a distal stone and rising LFTs. Overnight prior to this visit she reports an episode of rigors and subjective fever with RUQ pain that resolved by morning — concerning for evolving ascending cholangitis. She is followed by MFM. She confirms daily prenatal vitamin and reports she also takes low-dose aspirin 81 mg daily, started by MFM for preeclampsia prophylaxis (not currently on the clinic medication list); she refers to it as her 'blood thinner.' She reports a prior IV iodinated contrast reaction (pruritus and hives during a CT), not previously documented.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 37.0 C, HR 88, RR 16, BP 132/84, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 163 cm (5'4"), Wt 69 kg (152 lb), BMI 26.0. Labs (2026-07-16): total bilirubin 2.4 mg/dL (H), ALT 210 U/L (H), AST 168 U/L (H), alkaline phosphatase 265 U/L (H) — cholestatic pattern, rising. RUQ ultrasound (2026-07-14): cholelithiasis with dilated CBD (7 mm) and distal echogenic focus consistent with choledocholithiasis; single intrauterine pregnancy. Gestational age 24 weeks.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Symptomatic choledocholithiasis with obstructive/rising LFTs at 24 weeks gestation, now with an episode of rigors/fever concerning for early ascending cholangitis — proceed with URGENT ERCP; do not leave in routine outpatient queue.
- Pregnancy: coordinate with MFM and OB-experienced anesthesia; use fluoro-minimization protocol (lead shielding, wireless/no-fluoro or minimized-fluoro cannulation technique) to reduce fetal radiation.
- Iodinated contrast reaction (prior hives): plan minimized-contrast cholangiography and consider premedication per allergy protocol; add allergy to record.
- Medication reconciliation: add low-dose aspirin 81 mg daily (MFM-prescribed, prophylactic). Clarify with patient/team that low-dose ASA generally need not be held for ERCP with sphincterotomy; continue unless anesthesia/MFM advise otherwise.
- Continue prenatal vitamin.
- Counsel on urgent return for worsening fever, jaundice, persistent pain; scheduling and pre-procedure instructions to follow from the coordinating team.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• The gallstone blocking your bile duct and your rising liver blood tests
• Planning an urgent procedure (ERCP) to remove the blockage
• Keeping you and your baby safe during the procedure (you are 24 weeks pregnant)
• The chills/fever you had last night

Next steps
• We are scheduling your ERCP urgently — not in the routine line — with an anesthesia team experienced in pregnancy, and we will coordinate with your MFM doctor.
• During the procedure we will keep X-ray exposure to a minimum to protect your baby.
• We noted your prior reaction to CT/X-ray dye (itching and hives) and will plan around it.
• We added your low-dose aspirin (81 mg) to your record; you can keep taking it unless the team tells you otherwise, and keep taking your prenatal vitamin.
• Call or come in right away for fever, shaking chills, yellowing of your eyes or skin, or worsening pain.
